message:
fix(fcp-sdk,fcp-host): 429 carries default retry-after + connector inventory update is replace-not-merge

Two independent, co-arriving changes from sibling-agent work.

## fcp-sdk: map 429 → FcpError::RateLimited with default retry-after

`map_http_status(429, …)` previously returned

    FcpError::RateLimited { retry_after_ms: 0, violation: None }

which meant every caller that observed a 429 got an *immediate*
retry budget of zero ms — the error was technically a rate-limit but
carried no useful guidance, and retriers that honored the field would
hot-loop against the upstream. Wire up a real default:

    FcpError::RateLimited {
        retry_after_ms: default_rate_limit_retry_after_ms(),
        violation: None,
    }

with

    fn default_rate_limit_retry_after_ms() -> u64 {
        u64::try_from(
            crate::retry::default_rate_limit_retry_after().as_millis()
        ).unwrap_or(u64::MAX)
    }

so the retry-after is sourced from the single rate-limit default in
`crate::retry`, converted via `u64::try_from(Duration::as_millis())`
(which is `u128`) and saturated to `u64::MAX` on overflow. That keeps
the sdk's retry code and the map_http_status output agreeing on one
canonical budget.

Existing test `map_http_429_to_rate_limited` tightens from
`matches!(err, FcpError::RateLimited { .. })` (loose — accepted any
value, including the wrong zero) to

    matches!(err, FcpError::RateLimited { retry_after_ms, .. }
             if retry_after_ms == default_rate_limit_retry_after_ms())

so a regression back to zero surfaces immediately.

## fcp-host: connector_inventory_apply now replaces instead of merges

**Behaviour change — please read.**

`merge_connector_update(existing, incoming) -> ConnectorConfig`
implemented a field-level merge on connector-inventory `apply`
requests: fields that were empty/None in the incoming patch were
filled in from the existing row. Effectively HTTP-PATCH semantics
against the inventory row.

`replace_connector_update(existing, incoming) -> ConnectorConfig`
replaces it with

    let mut updated = incoming.clone();
    updated.id = existing.id.clone();
    updated

i.e. the incoming payload is treated as the full desired config;
the only field borrowed from `existing` is the stable `id`. Empty /
None fields in the payload now *explicitly clear* the corresponding
field on the existing row. Effectively HTTP-PUT semantics.

Why this is a deliberate semantic flip, not a regression:

- The prior merge made it impossible for an admin to *remove* any
  field via `apply` — setting `name: None` or `env: {}` silently
  preserved the old value, so you could never clear a stale
  description or wipe an env pair that was no longer needed.
- `apply` is already distinct from "create" (which uses a
  `ConnectorInventoryAddRequest`) and from "remove", so the
  "how do I reset a field" gap was real.
- The callsite in `connector_inventory_apply_handler` hasn't
  changed: it still indexes by id and writes the produced config
  back into `next_configs[target_index]`. No other code paths
  reference merge_connector_update (confirmed by
  `rg merge_connector_update` — zero hits after this change).

Regression coverage: new test
`connector_inventory_update_replaces_empty_fields` constructs an
existing config populated across every field and an incoming payload
with every field empty/None, asserts the resulting config carries
only the inherited `id` and empty values everywhere else. That locks
in the new semantic so a future refactor that accidentally
reintroduces merge semantics fails loudly.

Operationally: any caller that was relying on the implicit
"`apply` preserves fields I don't send" contract needs to send the
full desired config going forward. Because `apply` is an admin API
that is generally constructed by tooling that already has the full
config in hand (rather than by humans manually crafting patches),
the migration cost is expected to be low.
